summary: This notebook demonstrates how to achieve a competitive leaderboard score on the WSDM Cup multilingual chatbot arena task using only the provided competition data and a quantized Gemma-2 9B model. It details a complete inference pipeline that handles multilingual text preprocessing, custom prompt formatting, and length-based dynamic batching to optimize GPU memory. The author highlights the effectiveness of Test-Time Augmentation (TTA) by swapping response candidates and averaging predictions, alongside multi-GPU parallelization to accelerate inference. Readers should learn how to efficiently adapt a large language model for binary preference classification without external data or complex training loops.
competition_flow: Loads test data, preprocesses and tokenizes multilingual prompts and responses with custom formatting, runs inference using a 4-bit quantized Gemma-2 9B model with a LoRA adapter across two GPUs, applies TTA by averaging swapped predictions, and generates a CSV submission.
data_processing: Fills NaN values with empty strings
Truncates long texts using token offsets to preserve context and inserts '\n(snip)\n'
Applies TTA by swapping response_a and response_b
Sorts inputs by sequence length for dynamic padding
Splits batches by length thresholds (>1024 and <=1024) to enable larger batch sizes for shorter sequences
ensembling: Averages predictions from the original and TTA-swapped response pairs, and combines outputs from two GPU models and two batch size configurations.
insights: Custom prompt formatting with explicit tags improves model comprehension for preference tasks.
Swapping response candidates during inference effectively reduces prediction bias.
Sorting inputs by length and using dynamic padding significantly optimizes GPU memory and inference speed.
Splitting batches by length thresholds allows using larger batch sizes for shorter sequences, further accelerating inference.
